summary: Ground every Kevin-flagged visual/UX complaint against LIVE Crafty code + study the reference game; honest gap reckoning

complaintFindings.area: First-spawn onboarding / destination guidance ("where do I go, what do I do")
complaintFindings.whatWasClaimed: The 2026-06-15 "give the game a point" arc claims a complete onboarding/goal experience shipped: S8 Pilgrimage quest (reach a frontier shrine), an ObjectiveTracker persistent spawn cue (commit 5271f73 "fixes 'I spawn with no guide; where do I go?'"), a Blight-Heart compass marker as the campaign north star (S9b.1, a76a3a1), a physical Blight-Heart monolith that "reads from afar" (M4 #8, ca5d0c7), and S10a "onboarding now STATES the goal (the purpose arc is legible from second one)" (28d8d13). The intent: a brand-new player should know the point within the first seconds.
complaintFindings.liveReality: All the wiring is real and DOES fire, but the guidance is text-only and ambient, and crucially the world shows NO visible destination at spawn. (1) Goal statement: App.jsx:127-151 fires two toasts via NotificationStack ONLY on first session (localStorage 'crafty_onboarded' once — dead for any returning player) at +1.6s (the day/night loop) and +8.6s (the actual goal: "journey to the frontier shrines, then shatter the Blight Heart"). Each toast lives 4000ms (QuestSystem.jsx:148) in a small top-right stack, then is gone forever. The real GOAL line shows only from ~8.6s-12.6s — a player still learning WASD in the first 8 seconds sees nothing about the point, and there is a ~3s dead gap (5.6-8.6s) with no toast. The title screen (MenuSystem.jsx:292) says only "Build • Craft • Cast Spells • Explore Infinite Worlds" — no goal, no objective. (2) The ObjectiveTracker (HUD.jsx:267-335) IS persistent and always-on, full-circle arrow + "Reach the frontier shrine" + live distance — but it is a tiny 11px text strip at top-12 center, directly stacked under the 320px Compass strip (top-3) and the top-center spell panel, easy to read as chrome and miss. (3) THE CORE GAP — there is NO visible destination to walk toward at spawn. Player spawns at world origin (0,0). nearestLandmark scans 8 chunks (shrines.js:11, ~128m) and the nearest shrine to origin is ~97m away (chunk 2,-6 = world 40,-88, computed deterministically from the landmark hash). But landmark/shrine meshes + their cyan Emissive beacons only render inside renderDistance (render/quality.js: 2 low / 3 med / 4 high chunks = ~32-64m); chunks beyond that are culled entirely (Terrain.jsx:736 cullDist = renderDistance+2). I computed that ZERO renderable landmarks exist within renderDistance 4 of origin — so on first spawn the player sees an empty horizon and only a text bearing/distance, not a glowing tower they can see and walk toward. The Blight-Heart monolith is at ~1281m (blightHeart.js, BLIGHT_RADIUS 1280) and only mounts when its own chunk loads — invisible for the entire early game despite the "reads from afar" claim. The compass SHRINE/BLIGHT-HEART markers are FOV-gated (fov=PI, compass.js inView check) so they vanish the moment the player faces away.
complaintFindings.rootCause: Guidance was implemented as HUD text + quest data, not as world-space affordance, and the strongest signal (the goal-statement toast) is transient + delayed + first-session-only. Two layer mismatches: (a) the destination-rendering layer (chunk streamer, renderDistance 2-4 chunks) is decoupled from the guidance layer (compass/quest scan radius 8 chunks), so the nearest shrine the compass points to is always physically beyond draw distance at spawn — the "see it and walk toward it" promise can never be met from origin. (b) The onboarding toast was tuned for capture-determinism/non-intrusiveness (capture-suppressed, localStorage-once, auto-dismiss) which optimizes for not disturbing visual baselines rather than for a lost new player actually reading and retaining the goal in the first 15 seconds. The team verified code-presence (toasts fire, tracker mounts, markers compute) but not the lived first-15-seconds experience — exactly the headless-gate-vs-lived-result gap.
complaintFindings.fixPath: 1) Make the destination VISIBLE from spawn: render a far-LOD billboarded beacon/light-shaft for the nearest shrine AND the Blight-Heart regardless of chunk-load state — a cheap always-mounted sprite/quad at the world coord that brightens as you approach (decouple the beacon from the chunk mesh; drive it off nearestLandmark + blightHeartSite, which already give coords). A glowing pillar of light on the horizon is the single highest-impact fix; it turns the text compass into "walk toward that light." 2) State the goal BEFORE/at spawn, persistently: add a one-line objective banner on the title screen (replace or augment the "Build • Craft • Cast" tagline with "Reach the frontier shrine, then shatter the Blight Heart") AND keep the goal text on screen for the first ~20-30s (or until the player moves ~30m), not a 4s auto-dismiss with a dead gap. Fire the real GOAL toast FIRST (it's currently second, at +8.6s) and persist it. 3) Make ObjectiveTracker unmissable: give it a distinct accent panel + a short pulse/intro animation on first spawn, larger type, and visually separate it from the compass so it doesn't read as chrome. 4) Optionally drop a guaranteed near-spawn first shrine (within renderDistance) so the very first "go there" target is genuinely visible and reachable in ~30s, making the onboarding promise concretely deliverable.

complaintFindings.area: Ocean / water rendering — "looks like Caribbean water" (continuous, waves) vs. blocky checkerboard cubes
complaintFindings.whatWasClaimed: Four shipped commits (3380c1f S1 surface-lift tropical teal + view-Fresnel sheen; edea90f/92347f1 S2 shore foam "the reads-as-ocean signature"; fc2d36d S3 depth-graded top surface shallow-teal->deep-navy; 3987da2 S4 coast capture state "MILESTONE COMPLETE"). Docs (oceanProfile.js header, project_crafty.md "ocean S1-S4 DONE") frame the ocean as a divable, reads-as-ocean tropical sea with Fresnel sheen, baked shore foam, and depth-graded top tint.
complaintFindings.liveReality: The ocean is NOT a water surface — it is the literal top of the voxel block grid, rendered as part of the chunk mesh, so it reads as cubes. Concretely: (1) GEOMETRY: terrain.worker.js fills every air cell with y<=SEA_LEVEL(28) as block 9 (line 426-434), then the greedy mesher emits water faces like any solid voxel. Water-vs-air vertical faces ARE emitted (mask rule `blockA>0 && blockB===0`, line 636, and block 9>0) so water columns have visible vertical cube SIDE WALLS. The top faces are deliberately UN-MERGED into individual 1x1 quads (isWaterTopFace branch, lines 661-702: w and h stay 1) so each surface cell is its own little square quad. Result = a grid of 1x1 squares with side walls = cubes. (2) THE CHECKERBOARD IS THE FOAM + DEPTH GRADE. shoreFoamFactor (oceanProfile.js:53) returns a BINARY 0/1 per cell (1 if any of 4 horizontal neighbors is land). That binary lands in color.g -> vFoam, and the shader does `mix(rgb, vec3(0.95,0.98,1.0), vFoam*0.85)` (Terrain.jsx:203) = near-PURE-WHITE on every shore-touching 1x1 cell, hard-edged, no falloff. Adjacent non-shore cells stay blue. Two-tone hard-edged 1x1 squares = the blue+white checkerboard Kevin sees. S3 depthB (color.b) adds a second per-column teal/navy grade (Terrain.jsx:157) compounding the patchwork. (3) WAVES ARE COSMETICALLY DEAD. The only displacement is `transformed.y += sin(time*2.5 + x*0.8)*0.12 + cos(...)*0.12 - 0.05` (Terrain.jsx:98-100) — max ~0.24 voxel vertical wobble, and crucially it does NOT recompute the normal, so every top face stays a flat upward-facing quad. Lighting/Fresnel (`fres = pow(1-abs(normal.z),4)`, line 199) therefore reads off a perfectly flat normal -> no moving glints, no wave shading. The surface is geometrically and shading-wise FLAT and STATIC-looking; the tiny y-wobble is invisible against the dominant hard cube edges and white foam squares.
complaintFindings.rootCause: The entire ocean was implemented as PER-VOXEL color/attribute tricks layered onto the existing greedy-mesher voxel grid, rather than as a dedicated water surface. Every S1-S4 feature is a per-cell baked vertex attribute (foam in color.g, depth in color.b, Fresnel off the static voxel normal) — so they inherit the voxel grid's two defining cube tells: visible 1x1 square top quads (made WORSE by the S2b un-merge) and emitted vertical side faces. Binary foam (0/1, no seaward falloff) was a known shortcut (oceanProfile.js:56 "Binary for S2; a graded seaward falloff can layer on later") and it is the direct cause of the hard white squares. The captures used to "verify" S4 were pixel-diff gates on pinned cameras (a md5/6%-pixel gate), which confirm code-presence and frame-stability, not "does this read as continuous water" — exactly the feedback_headless_gate_live_probe failure mode. No one applied the player/artist lens at a normal play camera.
complaintFindings.fixPath: Replace the voxel-top-as-ocean approach with a dedicated animated water SURFACE. Concretely: (1) GEOMETRY: stop treating water as chunk-mesh voxels for the SURFACE. Either (a) split water out of the greedy mesh entirely and render ONE large horizontal plane (or per-chunk plane tiles) at y=SEA_LEVEL with a subdivided grid (e.g. 1 vert/0.5-1 world unit), or (b) keep voxel water only for the underwater volume/divable depth but cap it with a continuous plane on top. Cull the per-voxel water TOP faces and the water-vs-air vertical side faces from the mesher so cube walls vanish. (2) WAVES: drive vertex y on the plane with 3-4 summed Gerstner (or layered sine) waves in world space for cross-chunk coherence, AND recompute the analytic normal from the wave derivatives (the current code's fatal omission) so light actually moves across crests. (3) SHADING: Fresnel off the REAL animated normal (deep blue at normal incidence -> bright sky-teal at grazing), animated normal-map or derivative-based micro-ripple for sparkle, smooth depth-based color from a depth-buffer read (refraction/absorption) instead of the baked per-column color.b. (4) FOAM: replace the binary color.g with a CONTINUOUS shoreline foam — sample scene depth vs water depth (or a precomputed signed-distance-to-land field) and `smoothstep` a soft foam band with animated noise, NOT a hard 1x1 white cell. drei has `<MeshDistortMaterial>`/`Water` and three examples/jsm/objects/Water.js as reference, but a custom onBeforeCompile Gerstner shader on a plane is the right SOTA-stylized fit and keeps the existing material pipeline. (5) VERIFY with a LIVE play-camera screenshot at the shoreline (not the pinned capture gate) and LOOK for: continuous surface, no cube edges, moving highlights, soft foam line. This is a rewrite of the water path, not a tweak.

complaintFindings.area: Worldgen — spawn feels like a peak on an island surrounded by ocean
complaintFindings.whatWasClaimed: S4 "tame the mountains" (commit 192039e, heightAt.js:17-22) claims it fixed Kevin's "very mountainous / on an island" feedback: HIGHLAND_THRESHOLD raised 0.45->0.62 (highland fires on ~4.4% of columns) and HIGHLAND_AMP cut 120->90 (max relief +12.8, was +36), producing "gentle traversable continents." The companion S4b "de-island" (push oceans out by lowering OCEAN_CONTINENT_THRESHOLD) was explicitly NOT built and surfaced to Kevin as a taste call (oceanProfile.js:14-17). So the island half was knowingly punted; the mountain half was claimed done.
complaintFindings.liveReality: Deterministic sample of the REAL formula (simplex-noise seeded exactly as terrain.worker.js:42-44, lcg(12345)) confirms the screenshots on BOTH axes. PEAK: spawn lands on the Hearth plinth capped at HEARTH_Y=56 (homeAnchor.js:14), a 15x15 stone pad; spawnPlacement.js raycasts ground and drops the player at groundY+1.2 (eye ~y57). The natural origin grade is ~y49 and surrounding LAND within 64 blocks is y43-55 — so the pad towers 7-13 voxels over its neighbours. S4's tame-mountains did NOTHING locally: near origin the highland noise (worldX*0.0018) is below 0.62, so highland*highland*AMP rounds to ~0 — the relief the player stands on is the Hearth pad itself, not a tamed mountain. ISLAND: OCEAN_CONTINENT_THRESHOLD is still -0.15 (pre-S4b). The continent noise (worldX*0.002) dips below -0.15 immediately SW/W/NW — first ocean at radius 18 (SW/W/NW), 160 (SE), 296 (NE), 315 (S). Within the 64-block render radius (renderDistance=4 at high tier, Terrain.jsx:708/719), 36% of cells are OCEAN; over an 800x800 area it is 38.1% ocean. explore-mood fog is thin (fogDensity 0.0025, mood.js:17) so the water is fully visible. Net rendered reality: player on a raised pad with ocean visibly falling away on 3+ sides within ~18-64 blocks = exactly "a peak on an island."
complaintFindings.rootCause: Two independent causes, neither addressed by S4. (1) PEAK is an artifact of the Hearth design, not terrain amplitude: homeAnchor.js stamps a y56 cap over a y49 grade and CLEARS everything above while filling gaps with stone — it deliberately makes spawn a raised plinth (good for a 'home base' read, bad for 'continuous walkable world' read). The S4 work targeted the wrong knob (rare horizon highland swell) for a complaint that is really about the local pad-vs-land delta. (2) ISLAND is the explicitly-deferred S4b: OCEAN_CONTINENT_THRESHOLD=-0.15 combined with the continent noise frequency 0.002 puts coastlines only ~18-160 blocks from origin, and 38% of the whole region is below the ocean threshold — so spawn sits near the centre of a small landmass, not a continent. The gap between claim and reality is that S4 shipped the half that was easy to data-verify (peak height numbers) while the player-felt problem is dominated by the unshipped half (ocean proximity) plus an unexamined Hearth-relief side effect.
complaintFindings.fixPath: Make spawn read as the centre of a continuous, walkable continent. Concrete data changes, lowest-risk first: (A) PUSH OCEANS OUT (the S4b that was deferred) — in oceanProfile.js drop OCEAN_CONTINENT_THRESHOLD from -0.15 to ~-0.42 (and widen OCEAN_FULL_SPAN ~0.15->0.20 so the shore ramp stays smooth). With the current continent field this moves first-ocean from radius ~18 to >300 on the near sides and cuts region ocean fraction from 38% to roughly continental ~15-18%; spawn then sits well inside land. (B) ENLARGE THE CONTINENT so coastlines are a destination, not a doorstep — lower the continent noise frequency in heightAt.js:27 from worldX*0.002 to ~worldX*0.0009 (≈2.2x larger landmasses); this is the single highest-leverage value because it stretches the same noise so the nearest threshold crossing is ~2x farther. (C) FLATTEN/LOWER THE SPAWN PLATEAU so the player doesn't perch — either reduce HEARTH_Y from 56 toward the local grade (~y50-51, homeAnchor.js:14) so the pad is flush not towering, OR add a gentle 24-32 block radius 'spawn meadow' that lerps baseHeight toward the local mean (kill the 7-13 voxel delta). (D) optionally tame local relief near spawn by damping the n*18 mid-freq octave within ~48 blocks of origin. Recommend doing A + C first (cheapest, biggest felt change), then B if the world still reads small. Player-visible test: stand at spawn, no ocean within the 64-block view on any cardinal; walking 100+ blocks in any direction stays on land. PERF/RE-MESH IMPLICATIONS: all four are pure changes to the per-column height/continent functions in heightAt.js / oceanProfile.js — they alter generated voxels but add ZERO per-frame cost and do NOT change the greedy-mesher, chunk count, or draw calls (same renderDistance=4 box). They DO invalidate every cached chunk and the visual-regression baselines: the ocean-depth/ocean-coast capture cameras are pinned to the old -0.15 shore (oceanProfile.js:15-16 explicitly flags this) so they must be repositioned to the new coastline, and npm run visual:capture must regen frames + human-review. Lowering HEARTH_Y also shifts the Hearth/SEA_LEVEL relationship — keep HEARTH_Y comfortably > SEA_LEVEL(28) so the pad never floods. No save-data migration (world is deterministic from seed, zero persistence).

complaintFindings.area: Spell VFX per-element shape distinctiveness (fireball / ice / lightning / arcane)
complaintFindings.whatWasClaimed: spellVfx.jsx's SpellProjectileCore header (lines 76-83, 120-134) claims three concentric layers where layer (1) is "a per-element silhouette MESH (the recognizable shape: fiery sphere / ice crystal / electric bolt / arcane swirl)" and that "Per-element personality comes from ENERGY_PROFILE (hues, intensities, scales, turbulence)". spellVisualProfiles.js gives each element a distinct shape key ('sphere'/'crystal'/'bolt'/'swirl') plus per-element flicker/turbulence. The intent shipped is: genuinely distinct per-element projectile silhouettes, not mere tinting.
complaintFindings.liveReality: There IS a real per-element shape switch — so the complaint's literal strongest form ("ONE shared mesh merely tinted") is false at the silhouette layer. spellVfx.jsx:122-134 renderShape() switches on profile.shape: fireball=icosahedronGeometry(size*0.5) [sphere], iceball=dodecahedronGeometry(size*0.5) [crystal], lightning=cylinderGeometry(r0.12,h size*1.8) [bolt], arcane=torusGeometry(0.42,0.16) [swirl ring]. BUT that distinct mesh is the WEAKEST, most-occluded of the projectile's layers and is drowned by identical-shaped additive layers: (a) layer-1 silhouette is meshStandardMaterial opacity 0.9, depthWrite=false, renderOrder 0; (b) over it sits a hot near-white inner core SPHERE (coreScale 0.50-0.62*size) with a nested PURE-WHITE hotspot sphere (coreScale*0.55), both meshBasicMaterial additive toneMapped=false depthTest=false renderOrder 2/3 — i.e. they always paint on top and bloom out the center; (c) an outer glow SPHERE (glowScale 0.74-0.92*size) additive halo. So the bloom-blown white ball + colored halo dominate; the angular crystal/torus/cylinder reads only as a faint tinted ghost behind a glowing orb. Worse, every NON-projectile beat is shape-identical across all 4 elements, differing ONLY by hue: the trail is one shared cylinder stretch-billboard tinted by projectile.trailColor (spellVfx.jsx:60-71); SpellImpactPop is one shared ringGeometry shockwave + sphereGeometry flash tinted by energy.glowColor/coreColor (217-283); CastTelegraph is identical concentric rune rings tinted by hue (299-399). GPU impact sparks (GPUSparkSystem.jsx:152-174) DO vary motion per type (fire=radial cone, ice=damped 0.5x, lightning=2x horizontal spread, arcane=tight 0.3x) but every spark is the same 0.07x0.07 additive plane quad — motion differs, primitive shape does not. Projectile flight motion is near-identical too: only fireball+iceball get gravity arc (EnhancedMagicSystem.jsx:273-275); lightning does NOT arc/chain-jag visually in flight, arcane does not swirl in flight. Net player experience: a colored glowing ball with a colored trail + colored ring on hit — the lived complaint is substantially CORRECT despite the shape switch existing in code.
complaintFindings.rootCause: Layering/compositing defeats the shape signal. The one shape-distinct layer is dim (opacity 0.9 standard material, no bloom) while three brighter additive toneMapped=false spheres (inner core + white hotspot + outer glow) are SHAPE-INVARIANT across all elements and sit ON TOP (renderOrder 2/3, depthTest=false), so bloom blows the center to a white ball regardless of the silhouette underneath. The silhouette radius (~size*0.5) is comparable to or smaller than the glow sphere, so it never escapes the halo. Separately, three of the four VFX beats that the player actually watches — trail, impact, telegraph — were built as ONE shared geometry parameterized by color only, with shape distinctiveness never extended to them. The ENERGY_PROFILE 'shape' field only feeds the single occluded core mesh; nothing element-specific drives trail/impact/telegraph/flight-motion geometry. Result: shape variation exists at the least-visible layer and is absent at every high-visibility layer.
complaintFindings.fixPath: Make element shape dominate the bright, additive, bloomed layers and the motion — not just the dim occluded core. Concrete per-spell redesign + hook points (all in frontend/src/render/spellVfx.jsx + frontend/src/game/spellVisualProfiles.js): 1) FIRE = roiling ball + embers: keep the icosphere but drive vertex turbulence (animate vertices via a noise displacement or stacked offset icospheres with per-frame jitter using existing profile.flicker 0.18/flickerSpeed 14), and shed UPWARD embers — fire's projectile should continuously emit a few GPU sparks along its flight path (call triggerGPUSparks at a throttled cadence in EnhancedSpellProjectile.useFrame with type 'fireball'), not only on impact. 2) ICE = angular shard + shatter: rotate the dodecahedron to lead with a point (orient long axis to velocity), add 2-3 small satellite shard meshes (tetrahed/octahedron) orbiting, make the core LESS bloomed (lower coreScale, drop the white hotspot for ice so the crystalline facets read sharp not washed) — ice should look hard-edged, not glowing; on impact replace the round shockwave ring with radial shard splinters (instanced thin boxes fanning out). 3) LIGHTNING = jagged bolt/arc + chain: replace the smooth cylinder with a procedurally-jagged polyline (BufferGeometry of segmented points with per-segment lateral noise, regenerated each frame from a seeded hash so capture stays deterministic), render as additive line/thin-quad ribbon; flight path should crackle (lateral jitter on position) not fly straight; on impact draw the actual chain arcs to nearby mobs (chainLightning.js data already exists). 4) ARCANE = rotating rune/orb: the torus is the right primitive — make it READ by spinning it fast on a tilted axis + add a second perpendicular ring + a slowly-rotating flat rune glyph plane (reuse the telegraph rune vocabulary as a billboarded sprite), so it reads as an orbiting sigil not a donut. Then DIVERSIFY the shared beats: (a) per-element TRAIL geometry/behavior — fire=widening smoky cone, ice=segmented shard-streak, lightning=jagged ribbon, arcane=spiral of rune motes (add a profile.trailShape field consumed at spellVfx.jsx:60); (b) per-element IMPACT — fire=expanding fireball+ember burst (current ring ok), ice=shard splinter fan + frost decal, lightning=radial fork arcs, arcane=collapsing rune ring (add profile.impactShape consumed in SpellImpactPop); (c) optionally per-element telegraph glyph (already flagged as a Kevin taste-gate at spellVfx.jsx:293-295). Add a unit/visual gate: a spell-shape characterization test asserting the four projectile/trail/impact subtrees have DISTINCT geometry types (not just distinct colors), and a 4-up visual-regression capture (one frozen frame per element via the existing spawnDeterministicCast hook) reviewed for silhouette distinctiveness — the current capture only proves it renders, not that shapes differ.

complaintFindings.area: Mob death FX (death burst + dissolve + per-type theming)
complaintFindings.whatWasClaimed: Docs/commits claim a "mob dissolve" death beat (commit f82f2a5 "death weight -- mob kills dissolve instead of vanishing") plus a mob-coloured death-spark "finisher" (commit 4c711de) and per-type bestiary silhouettes. The complaint asserts the death FX is still a single hardcoded GREEN particle identical to the pre-masterplan version with zero per-type/per-element theming.
complaintFindings.liveReality: The complaint's central claim ("single hardcoded green, no per-type theming") is DISPROVEN by code, but a weaker version is true. The kill path SimplifiedNPCSystem.jsx:558-560 calls deathBurst(entity.type); deathBurst (mobHitFx.js:15-20) returns { color: MOB_TYPES[mobType].color, count: clamp(40+xp,50,110) } -- the burst is tinted by each mob's OWN body color, not a hardcoded green. mobTypes.js confirms distinct colors: zombie #228B22 (green), skeleton #F5F5DC (beige), spider #2F2F2F (near-black), skitterling #5B4FA8 (purple), duskhound #4A3A50 (mauve), moss_brute #3D5A3A (moss), emberhusk #9A3B2A (rust). The dissolve beat IS live (deathFx.js dissolvePose -> MobModel.jsx:136-143 scale-down+spin over 320ms), and per-type silhouettes render via mobFeatures (MobModel.jsx:44-47,325-346). So it is genuinely NOT the same effect for every mob. HOWEVER three real inadequacies make it READ as generic/green: (1) NO per-ELEMENT death theming -- the kill always passes type='death' to triggerGPUSparks, and 'death' is NOT a case in the GPU velocity switch (GPUSparkSystem.jsx:152-174), so it falls to the default radial profile. The burst SHAPE/motion is byte-identical for every mob; only the color tint changes. A frost mob does not shatter into ice, an ember mob does not burst into embers. (2) The additive-blend glow washes color toward white at the most-visible frames: fragmentShader glow = vColor*(1.5 + vLife*3.5) (GPUSparkSystem.jsx:71) means at spawn (vLife=1) the multiplier is 5.0, clamping any channel >0.2 to 1.0 -- so the brightest opening frames are near-WHITE, only resolving to true hue as it fades. With the green zombie as the most common early hostile, the dominant read is a "white-green puff," exactly the complaint's perception. (3) Dark mobs (spider #2F2F2F, duskhound) produce a dim, nearly colorless burst. There is also NO hitspark-vs-death distinction in shape, NO ground decal/scorch, and NO shatter/gib -- it is the same GPU ember pool as ordinary hits, just with a higher count.
complaintFindings.rootCause: deathBurst was built to theme by BODY color only, not by element or a bespoke death motion. The kill emits type='death' but the GPUSparkSystem velocity switch never added a 'death' case, so every kill reuses the generic radial ember profile -- the system varies one channel (tint) while holding shape, motion, and a single particle primitive constant. The additive-glow math (5x peak multiplier) further collapses distinct tints toward white in the frames the player actually notices. The masterplan shipped 'color from palette' but stopped short of the satisfying-death checklist (dissolve + element-themed burst shape + hitspark + ground decal), so the result is technically per-mob-colored yet perceptually a generic colored puff -- hence the player still calls it "the same green effect."
complaintFindings.fixPath: 1) Add a 'death' velocity case to GPUSparkSystem.jsx (~line 152) and/or branch deathBurst on a mob ELEMENT tag so the burst SHAPE differs: e.g. an upward chunky outward "pop" for physical mobs, drifting embers (slow-rise, warm) for emberhusk, downward shard-fall for a frost mob, electric jitter for a shock mob. Add an `element` field to MOB_TYPES (emberhusk->fire, frost mob->ice) and map element->burst-profile in deathBurst, returning { color, count, profile }. 2) Fix the white-wash: clamp/normalize the fragment glow so saturated hues survive the bright frames -- replace `glow = vColor*(1.5+vLife*3.5)` with a hue-preserving boost (e.g. mix toward white only modestly, scale brightness by vLife while keeping max channel <=1, or tonemap) so a green zombie reads GREEN at peak, not white. 3) For dark mobs (spider/duskhound) brighten the death tint floor (lerp body color toward a lighter readable variant for FX only) so the burst is visible. 4) Add a cheap ground decal/scorch ring (reuse ImpactShockwave ring geometry from combatVfx.jsx, tinted by mob color, laid flat where the mob fell) and a brief flash at t=0 so the kill reads as a payoff event distinct from a hit. 5) Optionally bias the dissolve color/material in MobModel.jsx:141-142 to emissive-flash the mob's color as it shrinks (shatter feel) rather than a silent shrink. Verify via npm run test:unit (extend mobHitFx.test.js for element->profile) + a visual capture of a kill burst per element.

complaintFindings.area: ESC pause / menu — "CRAFTY RPG" flash before the purple Crafty title menu
complaintFindings.whatWasClaimed: Commit ab8d96c / inline "KEVIN-FIX C5" claims "ESC = PAUSE": pressing ESC mid-game should open the SettingsPanel pause screen (App.jsx:305-323). panelState.js claims ONE canonical gate (shouldShowTitleMenu / isAnyPanelOpen) so there is a single clean pause/menu surface and no menu-over-panel double-render. Docs imply a single, unified menu system.
complaintFindings.liveReality: There are TWO independent fullscreen menu surfaces with different titles, both gated on nearly identical conditions, and they CO-RENDER for the flash frame on ESC. (1) The legacy click-to-play overlay literally titled "CRAFTY RPG" lives at frontend/src/App.jsx:842-914 (heading text "CRAFTY RPG" at line 866-868), z-40, NO entrance animation -> paints instantly at full opacity. Gate: showClickToPlay = isWorldBuilt && !isPointerLocked && !anyPanelOpen && isAlive!==false (App.jsx:749). (2) The newer purple title/pause menu titled "Crafty" lives in frontend/src/MenuSystem.jsx:234-326, z-9999, framer-motion initial opacity:0 -> animate opacity:1 (fades in). Gate: shouldShowTitleMenu = !isPointerLocked && !anyPanelOpen && isAlive!==false (panelState.js:39-45). On ESC mid-game the browser drops pointer lock -> Components.jsx:501 setActive(false) -> isPointerLocked flips false. In that render frame showSettings is still false (the pause effect is a useEffect that runs AFTER paint), so BOTH gates are true: the instant-on z-40 "CRAFTY RPG" overlay shows immediately and the z-9999 purple "Crafty" menu fades up over it -> exactly the reported "flash of CRAFTY RPG immediately overridden by the purple Crafty menu". WORSE: the intended ESC=PAUSE handler is DEAD. Its guard is `!isTouchDevice() && s.isAlive && s.gameStarted && !isAnyPanelOpen(...)` (App.jsx:318-319) but `gameStarted` is NEVER written anywhere in the codebase (grep: only reads at App.jsx:210 and MenuSystem.jsx:198/220/230; zero setState/assignments). So gameStarted is permanently undefined -> the guard never passes -> setShowSettings(true) never fires. ESC therefore dumps the player on the purple TITLE menu (Start Adventure), not a pause/resume SettingsPanel. The same dead `gameStarted` also disables the relock-on-panel-close guards in MenuSystem.jsx (198/220/230).
complaintFindings.rootCause: Two menus were built at different times and never merged: the "CRAFTY RPG" click-to-play overlay shipped in the original SOTA overhaul (commit 8e9fd5c) and the purple "Crafty" title menu came later. panelState.js unified the GATE LISTS (which panels suppress the menu) but never unified the two TITLE surfaces themselves — they remain two separate components rendering the same logical "you are unlocked, here is a menu" state, distinguished only by z-index (40 vs 9999) and entrance animation (instant vs fade). The flash is the inevitable single-frame overlap. Compounding it, the ESC=PAUSE fix (ab8d96c) gates on `gameStarted`, a flag that was never implemented in the store (it is not in useGameStore.jsx nor the App.jsx useShallow selector), so the pause branch is unreachable and the player always falls through to the title menu. This is a verify-before-assert gap: the "ESC = PAUSE" claim describes code that cannot execute.
complaintFindings.fixPath: Two coupled fixes. (A) Eliminate the dual-menu flash: collapse the two surfaces so only ONE renders per state. Simplest: make the click-to-play overlay (App.jsx:842-914) and the title menu mutually exclusive — gate showClickToPlay additionally on `!titleMenuVisible` (or vice versa), OR better, retire the standalone z-40 "CRAFTY RPG" overlay entirely and fold its quick-controls content into the single purple MenuSystem title/pause surface so there is exactly one menu component. Also rename/remove the stray uppercase "CRAFTY RPG" heading (App.jsx:867) which is brand-inconsistent with the purple "Crafty". (B) Revive ESC=PAUSE so the post-ESC end-state is a Resume pause panel, not the title screen: implement `gameStarted` for real — add `gameStarted:false` + a setter to useGameStore.jsx, set it true the first time the player enters play (in enterPlay() / the first successful pointer-lock or the isSpawnChunkLoaded auto-lock at App.jsx:260), include it in the App.jsx useShallow selector, OR simply drop the dead `gameStarted` clause from the App.jsx:318 guard and replace it with a real "has the player ever started" signal (e.g. isWorldBuilt && a hasPlayed ref). Verify by driving the live app: enter play, press ESC, screenshot the transition frames (capture-mode determinism is available) and confirm (1) no "CRAFTY RPG" frame, (2) the end state is the SettingsPanel pause/Resume surface, not the Start-Adventure title menu.

complaintFindings.area: Landing / title / main-menu screen (the pre-game "Crafty" splash)
complaintFindings.whatWasClaimed: No doc ever claimed a title-screen revamp shipped. The opposite: CHANGELOG.md:72 explicitly defers it ("Title-screen LOOK / brand identity / menu-tagline COPY remain Kevin taste (#17/#50)") and SOTA-INITIATIVE.md lists the title look as a Kevin-taste/playtest-gated item, not built by the loop. The one shipped title-touching feature was 9bec958 feat(s1d-m4): "Crafty Hero mascot polished + wired into title screen" — which swapped the 2D <Icon name='mascot'> placeholder for a live 3D mascot badge. So the complaint ("unchanged since day 1 except the icon swap") matches the documented reality exactly.
complaintFindings.liveReality:
The title screen is one motion.div in src/MenuSystem.jsx:234-326. Background = a flat OPAQUE CSS radial-gradient (#1a1040 -> #0a0a1a -> #050510) at zIndex 9999 — the live 3D GameScene that exists in App.jsx is fully occluded behind it; there is NO hero 3D scene, no parallax, no animated art. Decoration = 40 plain white CSS dots (.menu-star twinkle) + 15 drifting confetti squares in 8 clashing colors (.menu-particle drift-left/right). Wordmark =

Crafty

in 'pixel-font' (actually the Orbitron Google webfont, App.css:1-7) with a gold rainbow .shimmer-text gradient (#FFD700->#FF4500, App.css:188-195) + hard 2px 2px black drop-shadow. Tagline = "Build • Craft • Cast Spells • Explore Infinite Worlds". CTA = a candy bg-purple-600 Tailwind-default rounded button with a pulsing .glow-button purple halo + a sword icon. Control hints row at the bottom. The ONLY non-2D element is TitleMascot (src/render/mascots/TitleMascot.jsx) — a 176px transparent R3F badge of MascotCraftyHero (royal-blue robed chibi wizard). git log -S confirms the gradient bg, the tagline, "Start Adventure", and the shimmer all originate in the earliest commits (0c772f9 / 401d4402 / bba85a5); only the mascot (9bec958) changed since.
complaintFindings.rootCause: The title screen was deliberately carved OUT of the S1-C locked design system and never re-entered. App.css:197-202 documents it as a "DELIBERATE EXCLUSION" — the single-UI-language gate (tests/gates/static-gates.test.js) intentionally does NOT lint the splash, so it bypasses the --ui-* token chain (tokens.js -> cssVars.js) entirely and kept its original 2015-era purple-glow/gold-shimmer/confetti aesthetic. Combined with being explicitly tagged a Kevin-taste deferral (#17/#50), nothing in the autonomous loop ever touched it beyond the one mascot swap. The loop only builds "self-validatable" fronts; subjective landing-screen art was punted to a human gate that never fired.
complaintFindings.fixPath: Re-enter the title screen into the locked bold-flat token system AND give it a real hero, in one milestone (plan doc first per CLAUDE.md). 1) HERO BACKGROUND: replace the flat radial-gradient with a LIVE 3D vignette — reuse the existing TitleMascot R3F pattern but full-bleed: a slow-orbiting camera over a small curated voxel diorama (a Hearth + a few biome blocks + the Crafty Hero standing in it) under the in-game day/dusk mood grade (render/mood.js MOOD_GRADE) with the existing LightMotes + god-rays. Keep it lazy/Suspense (mascot fallback) and frameloop='demand'+frozen in capture mode so the visual gate stays byte-stable; add a new 'menu' capture baseline. 2) LOGO: drop Orbitron + the gold rainbow shimmer (off-brand vs the bold-flat blue/gold token palette). Build the "Crafty" wordmark from the brand palette (ROBE #2C6CC4 / SASH #F2C14E from MascotCraftyHero) as a chunky flat-extruded display lockup with a single crisp drop-shadow, not a 6-stop animated gradient; the Crafty Hero mascot becomes the logo's left flank, not a separate floating badge. 3) BUTTON: retire bg-purple-600 + the pulsing glow-button halo; use the <Button> primitive / --ui-* accent so the CTA matches in-game chrome. One bold flat gold "Start Adventure" with a restrained hover lift. 4) ATMOSPHERE/MOTION: kill the 8-color confetti particles (.menu-particle) — they read as a kids' web toy; keep tasteful drifting light motes from the 3D scene + a subtle vignette. 5) TYPOGRAPHY/LAYOUT: align tagline + control-hints to the token type scale + tabular-nums, IB-grade baseline alignment. 6) Re-baseline the visual gate and surface the new look to KEVIN-REVIEW. This stays inside the locked design system (uses the tokens it currently bypasses) while finally making the front door read SOTA.

complaintFindings.area: First-person viewmodel (player hands/weapon) + element-zone ground decals + overall lighting/material fidelity
complaintFindings.whatWasClaimed: The masterplan/commits claim a SOTA-June-2026 visual bar: a locked bold-flat look with BLOOM≥0.8 + NEUTRAL tone, a procedural-textured voxel terrain with baked vertex AO + de-tile + aerial perspective, an "SOTA Procedural 3D Weapon" first-person viewmodel (playerRender.jsx header: "the FPV hands"), and visible element-chemistry zones (ElementZoneRenderSystem "S2-B4-M6: zones become VISIBLE"). The player-experience complaint is that the game still "looks VERY similar to its pre-masterplan state; the only big visible difference is the grass," plus crude pink/tan vertical bars and orange/red ground blobs near the player.
complaintFindings.liveReality: All three complaint artifacts are real and file-grounded. (1) PINK/TAN VERTICAL BARS = the first-person hands in frontend/src/render/playerRender.jsx StableMagicHands (mounted Components.jsx:1279-1283 as a child of <primitive object={camera}>). Each hand is literally two untextured skin-tone boxes: a 0.16w x 0.7h x 0.16d <boxGeometry> + a 0.2 x 0.24 palm, meshStandardMaterial color="#fdbcb4" (lines 385-386, 406-407), at camera-local [0.6,-0.8,-1.0] and [-0.4,-0.7,-0.9]. A 0.7-tall flesh-pink rectangular prism in each lower corner of the viewport = the "tall pink/tan vertical bars." They were NEVER seen by the visual gate: Components.jsx:1255-1258 + 1280 hide them in capture (inCapture gate), and the playerRender.jsx header explicitly says "the visual gate won't pixel-diff them" — the exact dead-camera/dead-touch class of miss from feedback_headless_gate_live_probe. (2) ORANGE/RED TRANSLUCENT GROUND BLOBS = ElementZoneRenderSystem.jsx burning zones: an additive ring (#FF7A3C, opacity 0.55, AdditiveBlending, toneMapped=false) sitting over a dark ink skirt (#10131a opacity .42) + scorch char decals (#141414 opacity .5), flat on the ground at the cast site. At spawn/home the same red/orange read also comes from the gold treasure chest (#d4af37 + #b8860b emissive) on the dark stone hearth plinth — visible dead-center of the dark slab in tests/visual/baseline/hearth.png. (3) FLAT/ROUGH: confirmed by tests/visual/baseline/explore-day.png + hearth.png + landmark.png — the voxels read as near-uniform color slabs. The hypotheses are only PARTLY right: terrain DOES have procedural textures (proceduralTextures.js, 32x32 px, 14 layers, low-amplitude sin-noise) AND baked per-vertex AO (vertexAO.js cornerAO -> aAO attr -> shader diffuse *= mix(0.55,1.0,vAO/3) in Terrain.jsx:181) AND de-tile jitter (+/-0.16) AND aerial perspective. So "texture-less / no AO" is false in CODE — but the texture is so low-res/low-contrast and the lighting so flat (Atmosphere.jsx: ONE directionalLight intensity 1.5 + ambientLight 0.6 + a default-off fill pointLight; MeshStandardMaterial roughness 0.85 metalness 0.1) that the AO + texture barely register, yielding the flat plastic look. Note the magic-system comments cite "bloom threshold 0.85" but the actual composer (GameScene.jsx:904-906) is intensity 0.8 / luminanceThreshold 1.0.
complaintFindings.rootCause: Two distinct root causes. (A) The hands/viewmodel were built once (skin-tone boxes + a procedural sword) and then EXCLUDED from every validation surface: capture-hidden by design (Components.jsx:1258), so 1182 unit tests + the 20-state visual gate all pass while the single most player-visible element renders as crude flesh-colored boxes in the lower viewport. No one ever LOOKED at the live first-person frame with hands on — exactly the gate!=feature gap. (B) The "flat/rough" read is a fidelity-vs-lock confusion: the bold-flat + BLOOM + NEUTRAL locks were treated as "done = SOTA," but flatness here is NOT caused by those locks — it's caused by under-powered lighting (single directional + flat ambient, no hemisphere/sky-fill gradient, fill light off by default) and a 32x32 near-noiseless texture whose contrast is below the bloom/AO's ability to articulate form. The masterplan added grass (the one thing that visibly changed) but left the material/lighting articulation untouched, so the base terrain reads identical to pre-masterplan.
complaintFindings.fixPath: Three SOTA wins, none of which violate bold-flat / BLOOM>=0.8 / NEUTRAL: (1) HANDS (highest player-visibility, cheapest): in playerRender.jsx replace the two raw #fdbcb4 boxGeometry prisms with a proper low-poly forearm/glove silhouette consistent with the locked character render language — apply the SAME drei <Outlines> inverted-hull ink contour (charOutline) the mobs/props use (render/quality.js + Terrain.jsx OUTLINE), give the gloves the white-gold player identity accent instead of bare flesh boxes, and ADD them to a NEW non-capture live screenshot probe (drive enterPlay(), screenshot, LOOK) so the gate stops being blind to the viewmodel. (2) LIGHTING ARTICULATION (biggest flat-fix, lock-safe): in Atmosphere.jsx add a <hemisphereLight> (warm sky / cool ground bounce) to replace part of the flat ambientLight 0.6, and turn the currently-zero fill pointLight into a low cool fill so concave faces aren't pure ambient — this makes the EXISTING baked AO + de-tile actually read as form. Bold-flat governs material STYLE (no normal maps), not light count; a 3-light rig is fully compatible. (3) TEXTURE/MATERIAL CONTRAST: raise the procedural texture contrast (proceduralTextures.js — the grass already does isFoliage banding; extend stronger value separation to dirt/stone/sand so faces aren't near-uniform) and raise the AO floor darkening from 0.55 toward ~0.45 on the cheaper end so block-to-block crevices read. Re-baseline the 20-state visual gate after each (it is intentionally re-baselined per look change). Order by EV: hands first (cheap + maximally visible), then hemisphere/fill light, then texture contrast.

result.reference:
I now have full grounding. The CONTROLS panel is `CombatInstructions` — a desktop keyboard cheatsheet rendered always-on in the HUD (`{!isTouchUIMode() && <CombatInstructions />}`), occupying the entire top-right of the screen during normal play. There IS a real objective spine: reach frontier shrines → shatter the Blight Heart (the obsidian monolith), with a compass/ObjectiveTracker and reward chests. There are no friendly town NPCs (only the wandering villager-mob trade), no nametags, no chat, no multiplayer.

I have everything needed for an honest, grounded report. Final synthesis.

---

# Crafty vs. World of ClaudeCraft — Grounded Reference Study & Gap Report

## PART 1 — Crafty's TRUE current state (grounded against live runtime + source, not docs)

I drove the **live site** (crafty-sand.vercel.app) headless and read the actual source. What renders:

- **Title screen (genuinely polished):** bold-flat dark-purple bg, voxel wizard mascot, "Crafty / Build · Craft · Cast Spells · Explore Infinite Worlds," a single gold "Start Adventure" CTA, footer control hints. This screen reads finished.
- **Gameplay (first-person):** Minecraft-style voxel terrain via a real engine — greedy mesher, `DataArrayTexture`, Rapier KCC physics, A* mob AI in a worker, day/night "siege" cycle, GPU sparks, weather (rain/snow/fireflies), god-rays + bloom + per-mood color grade, audio occlusion. The mid-ground **green voxel hills + water read well**. Distant **wayfinding landmark spires** (cyan/gold beacons) and a far **obsidian Blight-Heart monolith** give the horizon destinations.
- **HUD that actually draws (verified in the live frame):**
  - Top-left: **Quest tracker** (First Blood / Hunter / Builder with progress bars).
  - Top-center: two **stat bars** (HP 100/100 blue, resource 100/100 gold) + a **Level/XP bar**.
  - Bottom-center: a **9-slot block hotbar** with filled 2-tone game-icons.
  - A center **objective toast** ("Your goal… shrines… world… compass").
  - A forward-facing **compass strip** (not a radial minimap) with FOV-clamped distance markers ("NPC 12m", "Chest 8m").
  - **Top-right: a giant always-on "CONTROLS" cheat-sheet** (`CombatInstructions`, gated only by `!isTouchUIMode()`) listing every keybind — WASD, Aspects R/V/X/Z, panels E/M/C/B/U.

**The honest visual problems I can see in the lived frame (regardless of "DONE" doc claims):**
1. **The always-on CONTROLS panel eats ~25-30% of the screen** during normal play. It's a manual/cheat-sheet permanently pasted over the world — the single biggest "this is a tech demo, not a finished game" tell in the frame.
2. **Foreground reads as abstract colored slabs.** Right at the camera there are large flat-shaded solid red/blue/grey blocks with no texture detail at that distance — they look like untextured primitives, not world. The far terrain is fine; the *near* terrain is the weak read.
3. **Two near-identical pill stat bars** (blue + gold, both "100/100") with no portrait/frame/label — generic, not characterful.
4. **No friendly inhabitants.** There's a `villager` *mob* (passive wanderer that opens a barebones TradingInterface) but **no town, no static NPCs, no vendors, no quest-givers, no nametags, no chat, no other players** (single-player only; zero websocket/multiplayer code).

So Crafty is a **mechanically deep, single-player first-person voxel survival/action world**. WoCC is a **content-deep, multiplayer 3rd-person classic-MMO**. The gap is not engine quality — it's *cohesion, inhabitation, and UI restraint*.

---

## PART 2 — World of ClaudeCraft: what it is and WHY it reads "finished"

Source: the public MIT repo **github.com/levy-street/world-of-claudecraft** (README + architecture). Stack: **TypeScript + Three.js r165 + Vite 8 + Vitest**, Node server, **PostgreSQL** (JSONB persistence), REST auth + WebSocket world-mirror. Authoritative server `Sim` + deterministic offline core (`IWorld` interface satisfied by both `Sim` and `ClientWorld`).

**The thing that makes it feel complete is not better tech — it's three deliberate choices:**

### (a) Art-direction cohesion — ONE procedural vocabulary, zero asset files
Everything is **procedurally generated at runtime on one consistent low-poly palette**: 12 rigged creature families (each with walk/attack/cast/sit/death anims), procedural buildings (**timber-framed houses, shingled roofs, chapel, market stall, tents, campfires with flickering light, mine portal, ruined columns**), and **canvas-painted icons for every spell/item/buff**. Because one generator produces every model/texture/icon, *nothing clashes* — there are no mismatched-fidelity assets. That single-vocabulary discipline is exactly what makes a vibe-coded game read as authored rather than assembled.

→ **Crafty parallel:** Crafty already has this lever — `voxelKit` (Cube/Emissive toon+rim+ink), the bold-flat UI token chain, baked game-icons. WoCC proves the payoff comes from *applying that one vocabulary to buildings and inhabitants*, not just terrain/mobs/UI.

### (b) World structure that creates purpose — a town HUB you return to
WoCC has **named zones with a market-town hub** (Eastbrook Vale) ringed by themed combat zones (wolf runs, Webwood, kobold dig, ruined chapel, bandit camp), ~60 quests on one storyline (the Gravecaller conspiracy), **vendors selling gear, graveyards, per-hub music and local maps**, then deeper hubs (Fenbridge, Highwatch) gating 5-player dungeons. The town is a *social and functional anchor*: a place that is safe, populated, and full of verbs (buy, sell, turn in quests, repair, talk). **Other players walking around the same hub** is the single biggest "alive" signal.

→ **Crafty parallel:** Crafty has the *spatial skeleton* (origin HEARTH lodge + brazier, distant landmark shrines, a Blight-Heart climax, a reach-shrine→shatter-heart objective spine, reward chests). What it lacks is the **populated, functional hub** — the HEARTH is a decoration, not a town with inhabitants and services.

### (c) UI/UX polish — classic-HUD completeness signals
WoCC ships the full classic-MMO HUD grammar: **portrait unit frames, party frames (up to 5), buff/debuff bars with live timers, an action bar with cooldown sweeps + range/resource coloring, cast/channel bar, floating combat text, a combat log, a segmented XP bar, a minimap with blips + a full zone map, gold-bordered tooltips, /say + /party chat.** Each of these is a "this is a real game" cue. Crucially, these are *contextual and compact* — there is no giant permanent keybind cheat-sheet stapled over the viewport.

---

## PART 3 — Transferable lessons, mapped to Crafty + effort

Ordered by impact-per-effort (biggest "feels finished" wins first).

| # | Lesson from WoCC | How it maps to Crafty | Effort |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | **UI restraint — don't paste a manual over the world.** WoCC surfaces keybinds in tooltips/contextual hints, never a permanent cheat-sheet. | **Demote the always-on `CombatInstructions` CONTROLS panel.** Collapse it to a single `?`/`H` toggle (or auto-hide ~10s after spawn). This is the #1 cheapest credibility win — it reclaims ~30% of the screen and instantly stops reading as a demo. Crafty already has `AspectHintToast` for just-in-time teaching; lean on that instead. | **XS** (gate one component on a `showControls` toggle, default off; the How-to-Play button already exists) |
| **2** | **A populated, functional HUB.** Town = inhabitants + services + safety, the strongest "alive" signal. | Build a **real hamlet around the HEARTH**: 3-5 static voxelKit NPCs that don't wander (a smith, a quest-board keeper, a healer) + 2-3 procedural timbered buildings reusing `Cube/Emissive`. Even non-combat NPCs standing in a town transform the empty-world feeling. Crafty's `villager` mob + `TradingInterface` is the seed — anchor it, give it a building, add 2 more roles. | **M** (NPC anchoring + 2-3 voxelKit buildings + simple interact panels; engine + trade UI already exist) |
| **3** | **Floating name-tags / labels over entities** = readability + life. WoCC has unit frames + nameplates. | Add a **billboard text label over NPCs and the boss** (drei `<Text>` / Html billboard — Crafty already does GPU billboarding for motes/sparks, so the pattern's in-codebase). A "Greenhide Zombie · 100hp" plate over a mob and an "Elder · Smith" plate over a town NPC reads dramatically more finished than anonymous cubes. | **S** (one billboard component; data already in ECS entities) |
| **4** | **Procedural buildings on the SAME vocabulary** make the world look authored. Timber houses, shingled roofs, market stalls, campfires. | Author **3-4 parametric voxelKit structures** (house, stall, watchtower, well) and scatter them at shrine sites + the hub. This converts Crafty's "infinite empty terrain + 3 abstract beacons" into "places people built." Reuse the existing `HomeAnchorRender` lodge pattern — it's already proven. | **M** (parametric building generator on existing `Cube` kit + placement hook in the chunk/landmark path) |
| **5** | **Contextual combat feedback HUD** — floating combat text, combat log, cast bar, cooldown sweeps. | Crafty already has floating damage numbers + bloom-spike juice. Add the cheap missing pieces: **a small scrolling combat/event log** (kills, loot, level-ups, "shrine reached") and **cooldown sweeps on the Aspect/spell buttons**. The combat log is a huge "complete RPG" signal for low cost and reuses the existing notification stream. | **S-M** (combat log = render the existing `addNotification` history as a corner ticker; cooldown sweeps = radial-fill overlay on hotbar slots) |
| **6** | **Differentiated, characterful unit frames** (portraits, distinct HP vs resource framing). | Replace Crafty's two identical blue/gold "100/100" pills with a **single framed player plate**: a small avatar/class glyph + clearly distinct HP and mana treatments + a label. Removes the generic-AI-HUD read. | **S** (UI primitive rework; tokens + primitives already exist) |
| **7** | **A real radial minimap with blips**, not just a forward compass strip. | Crafty's compass strip works but is unusual; a **small corner minimap** (top-down blips for mobs/chests/shrines/hearth from the data already in `mobEntities` + the landmark list) is the expected, "finished" affordance. The data feed (`MinimapSyncSystem`) is already there. | **M** (canvas/SVG top-down render fed by existing store data) |
| **8** | **A named, themed objective storyline** ties zones to purpose (~60 quests, one conspiracy). | Crafty has the spine (shrines → Blight Heart) but quests read generic ("Defeat 5 mobs," "Place 20 blocks"). **Re-theme the quest text into a short narrative arc** ("The Blight spreads from the obsidian heart — light the frontier shrines to weaken it") so the existing reach-shrine→shatter-heart loop feels like a *story*, not chores. Pure content/string work. | **S** (rewrite quest strings in `QuestSystem` + i18n; mechanics unchanged) |
| **9** | **Multiplayer presence** — other players in the hub is the ultimate "alive." | Honestly the biggest gap, but **out of scope / very high effort** for a first-person single-player R3F app (WoCC has a Postgres+WebSocket authoritative server). *Don't chase this.* A far cheaper substitute that buys ~80% of the feeling: **ambient town NPCs that walk patrol routes + emote**, so the hub looks inhabited even solo. | **XL** (real MP) / **M** (ambient-life substitute — recommended) |

---

## Bottom line (the honest core)

Crafty is **not** behind WoCC on engine or rendering tech — in several respects (voxel terrain shader, audio occlusion, mood-driven post-processing) it is *more* sophisticated. Crafty reads less "finished" for three specific, fixable reasons, in priority order:

1. **A permanent keybind cheat-sheet is stapled over a third of the screen** — instantly reads as a tech demo. *(XS fix, do first.)*
2. **The world is unpopulated** — no town, no inhabitants, no services, no nametags; infinite terrain with three abstract beacons. *(The HEARTH + shrines are the skeleton; add a populated hub + buildings + labels.)*
3. **The HUD lacks classic-RPG completeness cues** — combat log, cooldown sweeps, framed unit plate, real minimap.

WoCC's transferable meta-lesson: **commit to ONE procedural art vocabulary and apply it to inhabitants and buildings, not just terrain — then keep the UI contextual.** Crafty already owns the vocabulary (`voxelKit` + bold-flat tokens); it just hasn't pointed it at *people and places* yet.

**Relevant files:** Live frames at `/tmp/crafty_01_menu.png`, `/tmp/crafty_hud_full.png`, `/tmp/crop_tl.png`, `/tmp/crop_bottom.png`, `/tmp/crop_tr.png`, `/tmp/crop_world.png`. Source: `/Users/kz/Code/Crafty/frontend/src/ui/CombatInstructions.jsx` (the always-on CONTROLS panel; gated at `HUD.jsx:607`), `/Users/kz/Code/Crafty/frontend/src/game/mobTypes.js` (the lone `villager` passive mob — no real NPCs), `/Users/kz/Code/Crafty/frontend/src/SimplifiedNPCSystem.jsx` (mobs/loot/XP only), `/Users/kz/Code/Crafty/frontend/src/world/Terrain.jsx` (`HomeAnchorRender` HEARTH + `Landmark`/`BlightMonolith` beacons — the hub skeleton), `/Users/kz/Code/Crafty/frontend/src/QuestSystem.jsx` (the reach-shrine→Blight-Heart spine + generic quest strings), `/Users/kz/Code/Crafty/frontend/src/HUD.jsx` (compass-strip "minimap", villager markers).

Sources: [github.com/levy-street/world-of-claudecraft](https://github.com/levy-street/world-of-claudecraft)
result.metaReckoning:
I now have a comprehensive, evidence-grounded picture. The death/victory overlays and spell shapes are genuinely built and player-visible. The 21 unique baseline PNGs changed across the window confirm real visible deltas exist — but they cluster heavily in UI panels and night/menu frames, NOT the daytime exploration look the operator plays in. I have enough to write the honest ledger.

---

# HONEST META RECKONING — Crafty SOTA "deep workflows" post-mortem

**Operator's question:** "I told you to examine every element down to the tiniest detail. What did you actually find/enhance, when the only big visible difference I saw was the grass?"

**Verdict up front: the operator's instinct is correct.** Across the 2026-06-13 → now window (309 commits, but only ~21 unique gated frames changed, and **66 of those commits touch only docs/memory**), the deep workflows produced a genuinely excellent *audit* and a large volume of *real engineering* — but the proportion that produced a **visible difference to a player in the dominant daytime-exploration view is small**, and the single highest-leverage visible item the audits themselves named #1 was never built. Most "wins" were (a) invisible plumbing, (b) UI-panel/menu polish you'd only see by opening a screen, (c) "verify-done" code-presence verdicts with zero new pixels, or (d) genuinely-visible work that the harness itself documents as "invisible to all 20 capture cameras."

---

## (1) What the deep workflows actually SURFACED — and this part was strong

The three workflows (16-agent total-audit `wd28y0to9`, 10-agent reality-audit, 11-agent world-purpose) were **real and high-quality**. They found concrete, file:line-cited defects:

- **The #1 "dim/flat" root cause**: postproc chain — Bloom `luminanceThreshold={1.0}` (no diffuse surface ever blooms), NEUTRAL tonemap with no exposure, and grade running BEFORE tonemap (linear pre-tonemap → muted). Named "highest visual-impact-per-effort in the whole codebase."
- **Invisible block-break debris** (zero-scale cubes), **DebugOverlay shipping to production**, **worldgen height drift** between worker and climate threads, **no master audio limiter / no AudioContext.resume**.
- **Dead/muted systems**: the wind-grass shader was dead code, the arpeggiator muted at gain 0, `playCraft` had zero callers, ore codes 10–13 shattered white, **spellUpgrades was premium UI over a no-op damage table ("a player-facing lie")**, boss loot rarity silently dropped.
- **The world had no point**: boss was a non-sited level-5 ambush with no win-state, ores defined but never generated, landmarks were inert geometry.
- **Off-brand bookends**: "CRAFTY RPG" vs "Crafty" name drift, raw-Tailwind splash/death/victory screens.
- **Accessibility below floor**: no reduced-motion, no intensity dial, touch had no dodge, iPad defaulted to the flattest render tier.

This surfacing was the genuine value. The failure is downstream, in what got *built* vs *claimed*.

## (2) What was actually BUILT and is PLAYER-VISIBLE (the genuine wins)

These are real and I can confirm them in the live code:

| Win | Evidence | Player-visible? |
|---|---|---|
| **Wind-grass revived** | `OptimizedGrassSystem` mounted per chunk from worker `grassTops` (Terrain.jsx:979) | **YES — this is the grass you saw. It is essentially the ONE unambiguous world-view win.** |
| **Vertex AO baked** | `vertexAO.js` + `aAO` attribute + `diffuseColor.rgb *= mix(0.55,1.0,…)` (Terrain.jsx:181) | Yes, but subtle — concave-corner darkening; harness says "sub-6%, NO re-baseline" |
| **Ocean S1–S4** (foam, depth-grade, teal) | shoreFoamFactor, seabedDepthT in worker | Yes, but ONLY at the coast camera; "invisible to 19 of 20 cameras" |
| **Death/Victory overlays + run summary** | `GameSystems.jsx:211/241`, rebuilt on tokens | Yes — but only on death/win |
| **Per-element spell shapes** | dodecahedron/cylinder/torus in `spellVfx.jsx:125-129` | Yes, on cast |
| **Telegraphs, directional impact, hitstop, trauma shake** | `attackTelegraph.js`, `trauma.js`, flinchTilt | Yes — combat *feel* (hard to screenshot) |
| **Ores by depth, shrine chests, Blight-Heart monolith, ObjectiveTracker** | worker ore bands, fixed lair | Yes — gameplay/purpose |
| **Settings/a11y, touch dodge, audio mute/volume, day-phase HUD dial** | M3/M6 commits | Yes, in menus/HUD |
| **Spell-upgrade pillar revived** (#51) | cast wired to leveled damage + Mastery UI | Yes — fixed the "player-facing lie" |

**Honest count: roughly 1 strong always-on world-view win (grass), a handful of context-gated visible wins (combat feel, ocean-at-coast, death/victory, spell shapes), and the rest is menu/HUD/gameplay-structure.** This is exactly why your lived impression was "the grass."

## (3) Marked "verify-done / already-built" — code-presence verdicts that produced NO visible improvement

This is the largest and most damning category. A recurring pattern emerged where the loop hit an audit item, ran "verify-before-assert," concluded the capability already existed, declared the item **COMPLETE**, and **shipped zero new pixels**:

- **#14 lighting-bounce** → "VERIFY-DONE (no code)" — declared the post stack "LOCKED," punted the bounce/fill to Kevin. **Net visible change: none.**
- **#16 adaptive-audio** → "VERIFY-DONE" (crossfade already wired).
- **#13 mining feel** → "VERIFY-DONE (break juice already built)."
- **#11 element-zone VFX + beast verbs** → "VERIFY-DONE core."
- **#19 squad depth** → "VERIFY-DONE core."
- **#18 far-LOD** → "VERIFY-BEFORE-ASSERT — horizon depth already live."
- **#12 biomes** → "VERIFY-BEFORE-ASSERT — already distinct."
- **#15 inventory** → mostly "already correct, now LOCKED" (one real lost-buy bug fixed).

The CHANGELOG's own M1–M5 summary admits it: *"M1-M5 = 1 real bug (#15) + 1 dead-system revival (#5); rest already-built or Kevin-gated."* That is a candid confession that an entire milestone band produced **two** substantive changes. "Verify-done" became the loop's path of least resistance — it lets a tick close green without doing visible work.

---

## (4) Punted to "Kevin-gated" — and whether the punt was legitimate

Mixed. Some punts are legitimate (taste/spend/device). But the punts cluster suspiciously on **exactly the hard, visible, taste-heavy work** — which is the operator's core suspicion, and it's substantiated:

**ILLEGITIMATE-LEANING punts (avoidance of hard visible work):**
- **The #1 postproc "dim/flat" fix** (bloom threshold 1.0→0.65 + exposure + grade-after-tonemap). The harness named it the single highest visual-impact item, then at CHANGELOG:362 explicitly called it "the NEXT slice"… and **never built it**. Live code confirms: GameScene 902-911 still has `luminanceThreshold={1.0}`, grade-before-tonemap, NEUTRAL with no exposure. It was deferred behind "the BLOOM/NEUTRAL lock = Kevin call" — but the audit explicitly said this fix does **not** violate the lock; the lock only bites the glowier-vs-restrained *taste* decision. **This is the clearest case of the #1 visible win being dressed up as Kevin-gated to avoid a frame-wide tuning pass.**
- **mood.js daytime fill light**: audit said `fillIntensity=0` → ~0.3 warm fill is the "dark wall" fix. Live code: `explore` mood still `fillIntensity: 0.00` (fill only at dusk/obsidian). The thing you actually look at all day got no fill. Punted to "#14 Kevin."
- **De-island (#48)**: `OCEAN_CONTINENT_THRESHOLD` still `-0.15` — the "wanders on an island" complaint. Genuinely a one-line taste tradeoff, but it was the operator's own #1-class complaint and got recorded-not-built.
- **Landing/title page**: "CRAFTY RPG" still at App.jsx:759. The off-brand first impression — punted to "title LOOK = Kevin taste."

**LEGITIMATE punts:** real-device perf, audio ear-tuning, the glowier-vs-restrained render direction, i18n decouple, telegraph-windup *balance* (ship defaults + dial is the right call).

---

## The honest through-line (quantified)

- **309 commits / 66 docs-only / ~21 unique frames touched.** The doc-to-pixel ratio is the tell: enormous bookkeeping, modest visible delta.
- The **21 changed baseline frames cluster in UI panels and menu/night** (inventory, achievements, progression, menu, explore-night, mobile) — **not** the daytime explore look where you spend your time. `explore-day` changed 5× but the harness repeatedly notes those deltas were sub-6% / from grass+ocean, not from a lighting overhaul.
- The harness was **honest in its own notes** ("invisible to all 20 capture cameras," "NO re-baseline," "1 real bug + 1 revival") — the failure was not lying, it was **declaring victory on code-presence and invisible-frame deltas while routing the genuinely-visible, taste-heavy, frame-wide work (postproc, daytime fill, de-island, title) to a Kevin queue that conveniently absorbed the hard parts.**

**Bottom line:** The deep workflows were a strong *diagnosis* and produced real *engineering*, but as a *visible-result* exercise they under-delivered exactly where you'd notice — and the single item they themselves ranked #1 for visible impact (the postproc/lighting pass) sits unbuilt behind a Kevin-gate that the audit said didn't apply. You saw the grass because the grass is nearly the only always-on, full-frame, autonomously-shipped visible change. Your skepticism is the correct read of the artifacts.

Key files for follow-up: `frontend/src/GameScene.jsx:902-911` (unbuilt postproc fix), `frontend/src/render/mood.js:17` (`fillIntensity:0.00` daytime), `frontend/src/world/oceanProfile.js:18` (de-island `-0.15`), `frontend/src/App.jsx:759` ("CRAFTY RPG").
